original_hypothesis.title: Shared nonce across permit and transferWithAuthorization enables deterministic invalidation/griefing (without mempool reliance via multi-sig workflows)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ERC20MetaTxUpgradeable uses a single _nonces mapping for both permit() and transferWithAuthorization(). Any successful call to either consumes the next nonce, invalidating pre-signed messages of the other type at that nonce. In environments with off-chain signing queues (custody, batch signing, multi-party approval), an attacker who obtains any one valid signature can strategically submit it to invalidate other queued signatures.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The chain has no public mempool, reducing classic front-run griefing, but operationally this still matters for custody/relayer systems where attackers may have one signature (e.g., previously issued authorization) and can later invalidate pending approvals/transfers that were signed in advance with expected nonce sequencing.
deployment_assumptions: Users or custodians pre-sign multiple permit/TWA operations assuming independent nonce spaces or stable nonce ordering

Attacker can obtain at least one valid signature from the victim for either permit or TWA (not via phishing; e.g., previously authorized action, leaked signature in logs, or legitimate but later misused)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: ERC20MetaTxUpgradeable builds both struct hashes using _useNonce(owner/holder), consuming the same counter.

proof_of_signal.pattern: permit() and transferWithAuthorization() both call _useNonce(...) on the same _nonces mapping

preconditions: Victim has pre-signed at least two messages (permit and/or TWA) expecting a particular nonce value
Attacker can submit one valid signed message before the other is executed
Victim/relayer system does not re-sync nonce before submission

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Single sequential nonce space couples two different authorization flows.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Anytime pre-signed operations exist in off-chain queues.

operational_questions: Do relayers/custodians pre-sign and queue multiple EIP-712 actions for users?
Are signatures ever logged, stored, or shared between systems (increasing reuse risk)?
Is nonce re-synchronization performed before broadcast in the gasless pipeline?
validation_requirements: Model custody workflow with two pending signatures at same nonce and show invalidation
Review relayer implementation for nonce management and retries

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Obtain any valid victim signature for permit or TWA (from prior legitimate flow).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Submit it on-chain to consume the nonce.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Victim’s queued signature reverts due to nonce mismatch, causing operational DoS.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Low once a signature is available.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Failed approvals/transfers, batch settlement failures, relayer retries and user support burden.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low to medium; users must re-sign with updated nonce.
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Gasless and custody workflows using permit/TWA.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverts due to InvalidSignature/nonce mismatches correlated with recent successful permit/TWA calls.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Relayer sees rising failure rates for previously signed batches.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Nonce jumps without corresponding expected operation type.

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
